"The stablecoin requirements under Genius conflict with the requirements under MiCA, which is candidly nonsensical considering the whole point of crypto and stablecoins"

Katherine on the contradiction between Genius and MiCA that's frustrating global crypto companies

"The big difference here is they want to cover tokenized assets more generally, but they also want to cover stablecoins issued by non-EU stablecoin issuers. One source of frustration for anyone that works at a global crypto company is that the stablecoin requirements under Genius conflict with the requirements under MiCA"

"You have a slew of stablecoin issuers with either a single issuance model, I believe Circle has single issuance, versus a multi-issuance model like MoonPay, which has multiple entities in different jurisdictions issuing different stables. What MiCA is now saying is, sorry US and non-EU stablecoin issuers, you have to get in line, you're going to have comparable standards to the EU issuers"

"Everyone has a few months, till September I think, to provide input. So we won't see proposed regulatory revisions for at least a year probably. But what's happening in my mind is that Europe is kind of freaking out in response to the growth of tokenization and stables, and they're figuring out how to fix this"

tweets.text:
I think the labs are basically done on AI prose.

I don't think they'll get that much better from here. None of the labs are losing customers because of prose. They are already better than 99% of humans, and for the top 1%, they know how to edit AI prose. The models have genuinely landed in a basin of "very good writers" and yes, the models have tics, but I think we actually prefer that at a societal level.

Those tics may change after a while across model generations, like when Target changes its seasonal clothing line to not look like last year. It moves around a bit to not look cheap. But at a societal level, we will adapt and be able to identify mass produced clothes.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: AI writing is so good now, there are only a handful of idiosyncrasies left to point out. Those will vanish shortly.

I think this is 100% wrong.

Frontier AIs are genuinely very good writers.

You have just now seen so much of it that it has degraded how amazing it is. But you've also forgotten how bad normal human writing is, because nobody in their right mind is publishing un-AI-assisted prose anymore.

Go read a random scientific journal, or a random article on Medium from 4 years ago. It's so incredibly bad compared to what today's AIs can do.

thread_tweets.text:
Yes but the question is why they don't want to read AI written stuff.

Think of yourself: when you ask Claude to explain something to you, do you read its explanation and say "wow this is awful writing" or do you come away with "wow, that was an excellent explanation, way better than I'd get asking someone else" Because that's what I come away with 99% of the time.

The reason why we recoil at AI writing is rarely because the writing is actually bad. 

It's because it's lazy.

Because reading someone's writing is meant to create an empathetic window into a writer's mind, and the writer who is just proxying AI is implicitly saying "you are not worth my time."

tweets.text:
Two different capacity expansion announcements today across $TSEM and $AAOI.

Tower Semi, supported by the Japanese government, announced expansion of its 300mm Silicon Photonics (SiPho), Silicon Germanium (SiGe), and advanced packaging capabilities. 

Targeting $1.2B net profit, and $3.6B revenue in 2028 (with a ~$28B MC, that's ~23.3 2028 forward p/e).

AAOI announced the commencement of its buildout, roughly 400,000 square feet of manufacturing to make 800g/1.6T production go brrr. 

The overall expansion is also supported by the local city government in Texas. (they also $20.85M worth of subsidies from the State earlier).

This one isn't exactly as materially new as Tower, since AAOI originally announced these plans before. Just commencement of it. 

Regardless, glad to see both of these companies in their Anime training arc phase as they power up.

tweets.text:
The Tech Tree of Civilization has been achieved to these dominant driving factors:

> The first being humans mastering their environment through language and technology. All of which allows less cognitive work load to be spent on survival and instead cognitive capacity on technology. Humans need to spend less time on food, shelter and the environment and could spend more time on improving their environment which was a key characteristic of the agricultural revolution. 

> Infrastructure and automation that further removed humans from any of the systems they have built when it comes to manual labour. This was a key characteristic of the Industrial revolution where humans spent less time doing manual labour and shifted most of their efforts towards cognitive tasks which accelerated technological development.

> A large part of unlocking the tech tree was driven by war and facing adversaries. During the Great War of the 20th century this resulted in many era defining scientific breakthroughs such as atomic technology and the mass mobilization of the workforce showing factories/automation are the key to industrial power and supremacy.

> Now we stand at the precipice of automating cognitive labour in which systems that may exceed our own intelligence will be able to unlock future parts of the tech tree without the need for cognitive labour. Technology will proceed to advancement without at large the intervention of humans if we achieve AGI/ASI.

> Robotics is the final piece of the puzzle when it comes to any remaining human labour and the automation of our Industrial processes for anything a consumer will need.

The defining arc of human civilisation has been reducing the need for cognitive load for survival to be spent time on unlocking parts of the tech tree to improve our quality of life on Earth.

The question will become what will humans do with their time if this cognitive labour which we have so far leaned on is at large automated by systems more powerful than our cognitive aparatus. How can we live meaningful lives without falling into the leasure traps from the Information Age Industrial boom. 

My hope at large is the following. For the majority of history we have looked outward to ensure our own survival and we have rejected at large our inner world which holds knowledge and wisdom I believe collectively is untapped.

To understand what our place is within the universe, our own sentience, what meaning lies outside of labour and the deepest questions at it relates to our universe through physics and mathematics leading to a new enlightenment era. 

This would be my hope over the centuries to come.

Yesterday, our Nowcast IQ models held the SOFTEST US inflation forecast of anyone on the street. This morning, the BLS proved us right — and then some.

The scoreboard:

Consensus: -0.1% MoM headline, +0.2% core
Nowcast IQ: -0.27% headline, +0.13-0.14% core
Actual: -0.4% headline, 0.0% core

We were ~20bps below the entire street. Reality came in below even us — in exactly the direction we called, on exactly the components we flagged (gasoline demolition, tame core goods, medical care deflating, no food pass-through).

Why does this matter? Because Waller put a July HIKE on the table 24 hours before the print. Williams drew the line at 0.2% core. Our models said the print would come in UNDER that threshold with high conviction — and told our subscribers to position for the hike getting priced OUT, not in. That is exactly what is happening as I write.

This wasn't luck. It's the same machine that has kept our subscribers on the right side of the inflation cycle all year — built on high-frequency price data, not vibes, not consensus-hugging.

And the part I enjoy the most: this beat was delivered from a sunbed in Corsica. Wall Street has the PhDs, the terminals and the midtown offices. We have better models and a pool.

If you want the forecast BEFORE the print instead of the explanation after it, you know where to find us. Nowcast IQ is live for all Steno Research subscribers.

#macro #inflation #CPI #FederalReserve #nowcasting

tweets.text:
google $GOOG  is struggling to sell it's TPUs, 75% of chips neoclouds use are nvidias but theres a strong case for google winning over the next year: cost.

meta, uber, microsoft and many others have cut back on aggressive AI spending over the last few months, opting for cheaper models - this has saved them $10-100Ms

but the largest cost in using ai models comes from the underlying infrastructure used to serve it to customers: tpus are the cheaper (and more effective) alternative.

my guess is as inference becomes 50%+ of ai token usage, data centers dedicated to inference will purchase a shitload of tpus, growing googles chip marketshare to 10%+ of nvidia's

- the org would create a protocol for assessing new models 30 days before release, ai labs would be required to share model cards etc before.

- model review group composed of frontier ai researchers from labs + open source.

-ai industry will fund the effort in order to attract the smartest talent + pay for the compute to test new models throroughly.

- america should be the ones to create and own this as the leading country for AI growth.

- if a model is deemed a national security risk then model release will be slowed down.

basically sounds like what the government is already doing but in a more formal body like FINRA or the SEC and way more transparency,.

well written and worth the read
